summary: Japanese short story writer of the early 20th century, considered a master of the form. Best known for 'In a Grove' (seven conflicting testimonies about a samurai's death), 'Rashomon' (a man robs an old woman at a gate in Kyoto), and 'Hell Screen' (an artist watches his daughter burn to complete a painting). Also wrote 'The Spider's Thread,' 'Cogwheels,' 'The Nose,' and the novel Kappa. His suicide in 1927 was a significant cultural event; his suicide note is titled 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend.' Names Japan's most prestigious literary prize (the Akutagawa Prize). Adapted 'The Parable of the Onion' from The Brothers Karamazov into 'The Spider's Thread.' His stories were combined into Akira Kurosawa's film Rashomon.

works.name: "In a Grove"
works.description: A short story presented as a sequence of seven conflicting testimonies to the High Police Commissioner about the death of the samurai Takehiro. Witnesses include a traveling priest, a woodcutter, the bandit Tajomaru, Takehiro's wife Masago, and Takehiro's own ghost (who speaks through a medium and claims he committed suicide). A sudden gust of wind reveals the woman's inspiring resemblance to a Bodhisattva. The setting (a bamboo grove) and the plot were combined with 'Rashomon' by screenwriter Shinobu Hashimoto into Akira Kurosawa's film Rashomon. The 'Rashomon effect' describing contradictory accounts derives from this story. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Seven conflicting testimonies about a samurai's death; includes a woodcutter, priest, bandit Tajomaru, wife Masago, and the dead man's ghost through a medium
examples: Witnesses including a traveling priest and a woodcutter testify to a police commissioner in a story by this author comprised of seven conflicting accounts of a samurai's death
a corpse found next to a rope and a comb, whose spirit confesses his suicide through a medium

clues.clue: The dead samurai Takehiro claims through the medium that he committed suicide
examples: the ghost of a murdered man found in a bamboo grove delivers the last of seven testimonies
A spirit speaks through a medium to reveal how he died and the lies in the other character's testimonies

clues.clue: Tajomaru claims to have lured the victim by promising cheap goods like mirrors buried under a mountain
examples: A thief created by this author claims to have lured a victim by promising cheap goods like mirrors buried under a mountain

clues.clue: A sudden gust of wind reveals the woman's resemblance to a Bodhisattva; she later considers herself 'forsaken by Kannon'
examples: A sudden gust of wind reveals a woman's inspiring resemblance to a 'Bodhisattva'... that same woman to consider herself 'forsaken [...] by Kannon'

clues.clue: Combined with 'Rashomon' into Kurosawa's film Rashomon; the 'Rashomon effect' derives from this
examples: The setting of that story, a gate, was combined with the plot of a story by this man presenting four different viewpoints of a murder to create a Kurosawa film

works.name: "Rashomon"
works.description: A short story set at the Rashomon gate in Kyoto. A young man sheltering from the rain watches an old woman rip hairs from corpses to make wigs. She justifies herself by saying the dead woman had sold snake meat as dried fish. Disgusted but emboldened, the man steals her clothes and disappears into the night. The story ends with the woman 'staring down into the abyss of night.' The gate setting was used in Kurosawa's film Rashomon, combined with the plot of 'In a Grove.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: An old woman robs hair from corpses to make wigs; a man steals her clothes at a gate in Kyoto
examples: An old woman robs the hair from corpses to make wigs and has her clothes robbed by a young man sheltering from the rain
sees a woman stealing hair from dead bodies and ends up stealing her robe

clues.clue: The dead woman had sold snake meat as dried fish (the old woman's justification)
examples: a corpse of a woman who sold snake meat as fish has her hair stolen by an old woman
a dead woman who apparently once sold snake meat as dried fish

clues.clue: Story ends with the woman 'staring down into the abyss of night... unknowing and unknown'
examples: ended a story with a woman staring down into the 'abyss of night' and observing 'beyond this was only darkness... unknowing and unknown'

works.name: "Hell Screen"
works.description: A story about the artist Yoshihide, commissioned by Lord Horikawa to paint a hell screen. Yoshihide binds apprentices with chains and attacks them with a horned owl to study pained expressions ('Monkey-hide'). He creates Five Levels of Rebirth by studying a dead corpse. He requests that his employer burn a woman in a carriage to complete the painting; Lord Horikawa has Yoshihide's daughter Yuzuki and her pet monkey burned alive in the carriage instead. Yoshihide hangs himself after completing the painting. Yoshihide is drawn from a painter of the same name in the third scroll of the Uji Shui. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Yoshihide watches his daughter (and her pet monkey) burn alive in a carriage to complete the painting; then hangs himself
examples: an artist watches as his daughter and her pet monkey are burned alive in a carriage by Lord Horikawa to inspire the title artwork
A painter hangs himself after allowing his daughter and her pet monkey to burn to death in a carriage while finishing an object commissioned by a lord

clues.clue: Yoshihide ('Monkey-hide') binds apprentices with chains, attacks another with a horned owl / snake to study pained expressions
examples: created a character who binds one of his apprentices with chains and attacks another with a horned owl to study their pained expressions
a man nicknamed 'Monkey-hide' who menaces his apprentices with a snake and an owl to see their reactions

clues.clue: Yoshihide studies a dead corpse to create Five Levels of Rebirth
examples: a man who studies a dead corpse to create Five Levels of Rebirth

clues.clue: Commissioned by Lord Horikawa to paint the title screen
examples: The Lord of Horikawa commissions the title artwork from Yoshihide

clues.clue: Yoshihide is drawn from a painter of the same name in the third scroll of the Uji Shui
examples: drew his inspiration for this protagonist of 'Hell Screen' from a painter of the same name from the third scroll of the Uji Shui

works.name: "The Spider's Thread"
works.description: A story adapted from the 'Parable of the Onion' in Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov. The thief Kandata, whose one act of kindness in life was sparing a spider, is given a chance to climb out of Hell (the Pool of Blood / Blood Lake) using the title spider's thread. He fails when he selfishly claims the thread is his alone, causing it to break and him to fall back in. The Buddha sends him the thread through a lotus pond. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Kandata fails to climb out of Hell because he selfishly claims the thread is his alone; adapted from The Brothers Karamazov's parable of the onion
examples: the thief Kandata, who fails to escape hell with the title conveyance
retold the onion anecdote from The Brothers Karamazov as a story in which a character falls back into the Pool of Blood

clues.clue: Kandata's one act of kindness was sparing a spider; the Buddha sends the thread through a lotus pond
examples: a thief whose one act of kindness in life allows him a chance to climb out of Hell
one character sends another character a tool through a lotus pond to escape Blood Lake

works.name: "Cogwheels" / "Spinning Gears"
works.description: A semi-autobiographical, posthumously published story in which the hallucination-plagued narrator (Boku) sees translucent spinning cogwheels blocking his vision and raincoat-wearing men. He picks up Crime and Punishment but finds a misprinted Brothers Karamazov. He learns his arsonist brother-in-law jumped in front of a train while wearing a raincoat. He identifies a 'neighbourhood Swede' as August Strindberg, whose Inferno impressed him. The narrator is tormented by thoughts about Yoshihide's fate in 'Hell Screen.' The story ends with the narrator asking 'Isn't there someone who will be good enough to strangle me in my sleep?' Prefigures Akutagawa's own suicide. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Narrator hallucinates translucent/semi-transparent cogwheels spinning and blocking his vision; raincoat-clad figures appear
examples: the narrator hallucinates translucent versions of the title objects
the narrator is haunted by the title 'semi-transparent' objects

clues.clue: Ends with 'Isn't there someone who will be good enough to strangle me in my sleep?'

clues.clue: Narrator picks up Crime and Punishment but finds it misprinted as The Brothers Karamazov

works.name: "The Nose"
works.description: A story about the priest Zenchi Naigu, whose extremely long nose (five or six inches, hanging below his chin) is his source of constant embarrassment. He needs a two-foot-long stick to accompany him when eating. A disciple boils the nose and stomps on it until millet-like beads of fat are extracted, making it shrink. After the procedure, Naigu is unexpectedly ridiculed (people preferred him with the long nose). He feels satisfied 'as if he had copied out the Lotus Sutra.' He compares himself to Liu Bei from Romance of the Three Kingdoms. Written by a non-Russian author who shares the story title with Gogol. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Priest Zenchi Naigu has an extremely long nose; boiled and stomped on to extract fat beads and shrink it
examples: the priest Naigu, who has his disciple boil and stomp on the title entity to make it smaller
wrote about a long object that is boiled and stomped on before having millet-like beads of fat extracted

clues.clue: After the procedure, Naigu is unexpectedly ridiculed (people preferred the long nose)
examples: the priest unexpectedly finds himself the subject of ridicule after undergoing that painful procedure

clues.clue: Naigu compares himself to Liu Bei from Romance of the Three Kingdoms
examples: Father Zenchi compares himself to Liu Bei, a character from this 14th-century Chinese novel

clues.clue: Not to be confused with Gogol's 'The Nose'
examples: This non-Gogol author of 'The Nose'
This non-Russian author of 'The Nose'

works.name: Kappa
works.description: A late novel narrated by Patient 23 (a psychiatric patient) who falls down a hole while chasing Bag the fisherman and travels to the land of the kappa (Japanese water creatures). The kappa society asks their fetuses whether they want to be born (one tells his father 'I do not wish to be born'). Their society cannibalizes the unemployed, believes in eradicating heredity, and practices a religion called Lifeism/Viverism. One kappa philosopher Geeru admires philosophers who killed themselves. Krabach leaves jubilantly after composing the perfect funeral dirge for the poet Tok, whom 17 people later interview. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Kappa society asks fetuses if they want to be born; Patient 23 narrates from an asylum

clues.clue: Krabach composes the perfect funeral dirge for the poet Tok; 17 people interview Tok's soul

works.name: "Yam Gruel" / "Imogayu"
works.description: A story about the red-nosed, low-ranking official Goi, who aspires to nothing more than eating his fill of the title foodstuff yam gruel. A man devoted his life to this desire. The story ends with Goi losing his appetite for the title food after having his wish fulfilled. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The red-nosed Goi aspires to eat his fill of yam gruel; loses his appetite after getting his wish
examples: an unhappy red nosed man aspires to nothing more than eating his favorite food, but remains unhappy after having that wish fulfilled
Goi wants to eat his fill of yam gruel

works.name: "Dragon: the Old Potter's Tale"
works.description: A story in which the long-nosed monk E'in erects a sign near Sarusawa Pond saying that a dragon will ascend to heaven on the third day of the third month. He plays it as a practical joke, but a mass delusion occurs and E'in ends up believing it himself when he sees a shadowy figure resembling a dragon. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Monk E'in erects a sign saying a dragon will ascend from Sarusawa Pond; a mass delusion results and E'in himself begins to believe
examples: E'in erects a sign near a pond stating that the title animal will 'ascend to heaven'
a man plays a practical joke by erecting a sign saying that a creature will ascend from a pond into heaven, and then ends up believing this

works.name: Suicide Note / "A Note to a Certain Old Friend" / "A Fool's Life"
works.description: Akutagawa's suicide in 1927 was a major cultural event. His suicide note, titled 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend,' describes 'diseased nerves, lucid as ice' and discusses his 'vague sense of unease towards the future.' Its postscript recalls discussing 'Empedocles on Etna' under linden trees and feeling 'I was one who fancied himself a god.' He states he does 'not regard suicide as a sin, as Westerners do' and concludes that 'use of a drug seemed to be perhaps the most satisfactory way.' He references Kleist and Racine as predecessors. 'A Fool's Life' is a last work of 51 prose vignettes set at a bookstore, including the aphorism 'life is not worth a single line of Baudelaire.' Kawabata quoted the suicide note in his Nobel lecture. Miyamoto Kenji discussed the impact in 'The Literature of Decline.' Osamu Dazai's No Longer Human may have been written in response to Akutagawa's death.

clues.clue: Suicide note titled 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend'; describes 'diseased nerves, lucid as ice'
examples: a document titled 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend'
'The world I am now in is one of diseased nerves, lucid as ice'

clues.clue: Postscript recalls discussing 'Empedocles on Etna' under linden trees; 'I was one who fancied himself a god'

The postscriptum recalls when the author and the addressee discussed 'Empedocles on Etna'

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Includes 'The Death of a Disciple' / the Lorenzo story (monk Lorenzo ostracized for having a child with an umbrella-maker's daughter, but revealed to be a woman after saving the child from a fire), 'Horse Legs' (Oshino Hanzaburo dies and is sent back to life with a horse's legs after officials kill him by mistake instead of Henry Barrett), 'Mandarins' (the narrator sees a country girl tossing mandarin oranges out of a train to her waiting brothers), 'The Christ of Nanking' (prostitute Song Jinhua cured of syphilis by a man pretending to be Jesus), 'Autumn Mountain' (a man sees the greatest painting ever but fails to purchase it), 'Shogun' (fictionalized General Nogi), and the story 'A Story of a Head that Fell Off' (inspired by Pu Songling's Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio).

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Japanese short story writer (1892-1927). Names Japan's most prestigious literary prize (the Akutagawa Prize, semi-annual). His suicide in 1927 was a major cultural event. Had a longstanding debate with Tanizaki Jun'ichiro over the importance of plot in literature. His stories were inspired by diverse sources including The Brothers Karamazov, Pu Songling's Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio, and the Uji Shui. His son Yasushi Akutagawa composed film scores. The title assassin in Jim Jarmusch's Ghost Dog steals a collection of his fiction during a mob hit. A popular COVID-era book club spent 85 days reading Tolstoy, but Akutagawa is also noted for being read in book club contexts. Kurosawa's film Rashomon adapted his stories.

clues.clue: Names Japan's most prestigious literary prize (the Akutagawa Prize)

This namesake of Japan's most prestigious literary award

comprehensive_summary:
Ryunosuke Akutagawa is one of the most heavily clued non-Western literature authors in quizbowl. His stories generate a remarkably dense and consistent set of clues.

'In a Grove' is his most frequently referenced work, recognizable by its structure of seven conflicting testimonies to the High Police Commissioner about the death of the samurai Takehiro. The witnesses include a woodcutter, a traveling priest, the bandit Tajomaru (who claims to have lured the victim with promises of cheap mirrors), Takehiro's wife Masago (who appears as a Bodhisattva figure but later considers herself 'forsaken by Kannon'), and Takehiro's own ghost speaking through a medium, who claims he committed suicide. This story's plot was combined with the setting of 'Rashomon' (the gate in Kyoto) by screenwriter Shinobu Hashimoto into Kurosawa's film Rashomon, giving rise to the 'Rashomon effect' describing contradictory accounts.

'Rashomon' itself features a man sheltering from rain at the gate who watches an old woman rip hair from corpses to make wigs. The old woman justifies herself by saying the dead woman sold snake meat as dried fish. Disgusted, the man steals the old woman's clothes. 'Hell Screen' depicts the artist Yoshihide ('Monkey-hide'), who binds apprentices with chains and attacks them with owls and snakes to study their pained expressions. Lord Horikawa commissions him to paint a hell screen; Yoshihide's daughter and her pet monkey are burned alive in a carriage as inspiration, after which Yoshihide hangs himself. The character derives from the Uji Shui.

'The Spider's Thread' adapts the parable of the onion from The Brothers Karamazov: the thief Kandata, whose one act of kindness was sparing a spider, is given a chance to climb out of Hell using a spider's thread sent by the Buddha through a lotus pond, but falls back when he selfishly claims the thread as his alone. 'Cogwheels' / 'Spinning Gears' is a semi-autobiographical story in which the narrator hallucinates translucent spinning cogwheels, encounters raincoat-wearing figures, picks up Crime and Punishment but finds a misprinted Brothers Karamazov, and ends by asking 'Isn't there someone who will be good enough to strangle me in my sleep?' His arsonist brother-in-law is hit by a train. The story prefigures Akutagawa's own suicide.

'The Nose' features the priest Zenchi Naigu, whose enormously long nose is boiled, stomped on, and has millet-like fat beads extracted to shrink it, but he is then ridiculed for looking normal. Naigu compares himself to Liu Bei from Romance of the Three Kingdoms. Kappa is a late novel narrated by psychiatric Patient 23 who visits a kappa society where fetuses choose whether to be born, the unemployed are cannibalized, and Viverism/Lifeism is practiced. 'Yam Gruel' follows the red-nosed Goi, who dreams of eating his fill of yam gruel but loses his appetite after his wish is fulfilled. 'Dragon' features the long-nosed monk E'in who causes a mass delusion by posting a sign near Sarusawa Pond saying a dragon will ascend.

Akutagawa's suicide in 1927 is a major biographical anchor. His suicide note, 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend,' references 'diseased nerves, lucid as ice,' recalls discussing 'Empedocles on Etna' under linden trees, and references Kleist and Racine. 'A Fool's Life' is a set of 51 vignettes including 'life is not worth a single line of Baudelaire.' Kawabata quoted the suicide note in his Nobel lecture. The Akutagawa Prize, Japan's most prestigious literary award, is named after him.
recursive_suggestions: Rashomon (film) — Kurosawa's film is frequently clued as its own answerline
Jun'ichiro Tanizaki — longstanding debate partner; frequently paired
Natsume Soseki — frequently appears in Japanese literature bonuses alongside Akutagawa
Osamu Dazai — No Longer Human may have been a response to Akutagawa's death
The Brothers Karamazov — source for 'The Spider's Thread'
